workInformation.title: Feels Like Power: Immersive VR Poems
workInformation.workDescription:
Feels Like Power is a collection of immersive VR poems set inside spherical photographs; thus, they fill what poetry ordinarily regards as whitespace with panoramic imagery. The poems are about the long afterlives of the Pacific war. They are set primarily in and around U.S. military bases in Japan, Korea, Guam, Singapore, and the Philippines, but also include locations in the U.S. and in cyberspace. When you read the poems in 3D, the poems, and their environments, extend panoramically around, above, and below you. By moving your head and body, you can navigate the environment and the text within it. The website enables VR within each poem for those who have VR headsets, but also features 2D versions which are accessible simply with a phone, tablet, or laptop. The project invites you to experience poems spatially in each site, and to travel between sites to discover resonances and contrasts.

In their form, the poems recall the popular 19th century panoramic installation paintings of war scenes and distant landscapes a viewer would likely never experience in person. 360˚ panoramas, then and now, promise the imperial confidence of total vision, the ability to see and therefore discover everything available to be known in a scene. But of course they are also merely spheres with finite edges, trapped in the instant the camera shutter clicks. In writing these poems, I hope to leverage immersive VR's affective power in order to learn about the limits of vision from inside an ideological bubble, about the capacity of poetry to see with depth, and about the traces of war still palpable in the mundane environments and language around us. What can we learn about war, about its overlap with domestic life, about history and humanity, from bringing these distinct places with their distinct histories into contact with each other?

creatorMetadataInformation.creatorBiography: Collier Nogues is Assistant Professor of Creative Writing at the Chinese University of Hong Kong. She writes at the intersection of digital and documentary poetics, with an emphasis on making connections across decolonization and demilitarization movements in the U.S. and in the Pacific. Her poetry collections include the hybrid print/interactive volume The Ground I Stand On Is Not My Ground (Drunken Boat, 2015) and On the Other Side, Blue (Four Way, 2011). Her creative and scholarly work has been supported by fellowships from the MacDowell Colony, the Ucross Foundation, Vermont Studio Center, and the Hong Kong Research Grants Council, and her practice-based research has earned the Research Grant Council's Early Career Award. She is a lead collaborator in DOKYU, which gathers artists, writers, and historians to explore transdisciplinary approaches to archives.

workInformation.title: Digital T[H]Readings [1994-2025]
workInformation.workDescription:
Digital T[H]Readings [1994-2025] is an interactive browser-native archive-work that treats documentation as a literary form: authored, navigable, and meant to be read rather than merely referenced. Structured as an expandable chronology, it invites viewers to traverse three decades of electronic literature and internet art through dense year-by-year nodes. Each entry pairs core metadata (medium, context, collaborators) with an "About" layer that performs a compact critical narration: how a work functioned in its original technocultural moment, what kinds of networked communities carried it, and what remains when those conditions dissolve.

Where many archives hide breakdown, Digital T[H]Readings keeps it visible. Dead links, vanished hosts, deprecated formats, shifting browser standards, and platform migration are surfaced as part of the record, turning obsolescence into a readable condition rather than a silent technical failure. In the ELO2026 context of (Un)Supervised, the work foregrounds how digital culture is continually "supervised" by commercial infrastructures and automated gatekeeping, while preservation and access-making often happen through unglamorous, human-scale labor: tending, narrating, re-linking, and re-situating.

The interface supports both linear traversal and associative drift, allowing history to be encountered as sequence or recombination. Embedded screenshots and contextual notes operate as micro-traversals, enabling encounter even when original environments no longer run. Digital T[H]Readings thus functions as both archive and artwork: a map of migrating networks and techno-communities, and a model for archiving electronic literature without de-authoring it. It asks readers to notice who gets excluded when access is supervised and how community persists through repair, not scale alone.

creatorMetadataInformation.creatorBiography: Mez Breeze is an Australian digital artist and writer whose innovative work has shaped electronic literature, net art, and interactive narrative since the 1990s. Renowned for creating mezangelle, a pioneering language that merges natural language and code, she expands the boundaries of digital storytelling and immersive art. Her projects encompass virtual reality (VR), augmented reality (AR), games, and AI art, including award-winning works like All the Delicate Duplicates and Perpetual Nomads. Mez's creations have been exhibited internationally and are featured in numerous academic publications. She has collaborated with leading technology companies and continues to influence the field through her exploration of synthetic environments and interactive projects.

workInformation.title: What Happened: Indexed Evidence
workInformation.workDescription:
What Happened: Indexed Evidence is a Twine hypertext memoir structured as the backmatter index of a book that does not exist. The work documents intimate partner abuse through the form of its aftermath: an index compiled in retrospect, in language built after survival rather than during it.

Navigation follows "see also" cross-references, replicating the associative, non-linear logic of trauma. Some paths resolve. Others terminate without exit. An input field accepts text and returns nothing. A second-person interrogation demands to know where you were and who you were with. Twine's linked, nonlinear architecture is the argument. The platform's passage-based structure, in which every node connects outward but no path leads to a recoverable center, formally enacts what the index's content describes: a system of cross-references that circle meaning without arriving at it. The form not only illustrates the experience of surviving abuse but also replicates its logic.

The piece draws on several intersecting frameworks in electronic literature and digital memoir. Scott Rettberg's Fin du Monde, a multimodal work published in The Digital Review combining AI-generated images and music to narrate a post-human apocalypse, introduces cyborg authorship as a framework for understanding how digital processes surface a cultural unconscious. This work grounds the project's treatment of survival language as data: indexed, cross-referenced, and ultimately irrecoverable in its totality.

Christine Wilks' Voices, an interactive digital fiction developed through the "Writing New Bodies" research project and exhibited at ELO 2023, withholds its subject's body entirely, generating meaning through deliberate absence. This work informs this piece's central formal choice that only the effects of what happened are indexed, never the event itself.

Lisa Farrell's "Daemons and Ghosts," an autotheoretical research log published in The Digital Review documenting AI collaboration on fiction, traces the experience of editing oneself into "only a character." That perspective shapes the autoethnographic logic here, where indexing becomes a technology of self-protection.

Laura Okkema's dissertation Hearing the Voices of the Deserters argues that hypertext authors exercise more control than print authors by coding every possible outcome, making constrained navigation a rhetorical force rather than a freedom. Such thinking drives the structural logic of this hypertext: every "see also" returns to the same place, and the reader who believes they are navigating freely is not.

The piece's formal argument is in the replication: abuse is structure. The entry that would explain everything is REDACTED. It was never indexed, but the reader searches for it anyway. The index that could not be written while it was happening is completed, finally, by a stranger clicking through it.

creatorMetadataInformation.creatorBiography: Nicole Patino is a writer, educator, and graduate student at the University of Central Florida. Her work sits at the intersection of digital humanities, electronic literature, and creative nonfiction. She holds an MFA in Creative Nonfiction and teaches composition at Valencia College. Her creative and scholarly work explores trauma, form, and the limits of narrative.

workInformation.title: GAIA
workInformation.workDescription:
Gaia is an interactive narrative that explores the fragility of our reality. It uses procedurally generated content by combining audio, image, and text progressions to ensure every read through is unique while maintaining a core narrative trajectory. A few key decision points lead to different endings. Gaia is available at https://mosher.art/gaia

The work references the lurking feeling that every decision we make leads to a split in reality, spawning an infinite multitude of paths not taken. It asks: what would it be like to navigate those paths? to be able to step through the lost branches of the multiverse? Throughout the narrative these differing entanglements spin in and out of existence creating a kaleidoscope of black and white line drawings. While the interior setting of a recreational vehicle remains consistent, the figures in the scene come and go, their audible chatter mixing with the inner written thoughts of the narrator/reader. Diffuse clouds of text swirl around decision points hinting and future outcomes. The primary mechanic, spinning, offers not so much a decision point but an opportunity to jump between the paths as the wayfinding mechanics are slowly revealed. If you could change a past decision you made, would you? And what would you take with you to help guide your way back to the present?

The public is invited to play through the Gaia narrative. Yellow text indicates clickable links to progress the story. Gaia is written and narrated in English. Average interaction time is 3 minutes.

creatorMetadataInformation.creatorBiography:
Matthew Mosher is an intermedia artist, research professor, and Fulbright Scholar who creates embodied experiential systems. Their work explores the intersections of fine art, computer programming, and critical making resulting in immersive installations, interactive sculptures, post-participatory data visualizations, and dynamic performances. Their projects have engaged themes of meditation, gun violence, digital isolation, and tangible memory. Mosher creates conduits between digital technology and material forms to highlight our complex relationships with machines and each other. Doing so empowers participants in their work to see the world from a new perspective while reexamining their role in society.

Mosher is currently an Associate Professor of Games and Interactive Media at the University of Central Florida. They received their BFA in Furniture Design from the Rhode Island School of Design in 2006 and their MFA in Intermedia from Arizona State University in 2012. Mosher has exhibited at numerous international venues for contemporary art, including the International Symposium for Electronic Art, Electronic Literature Organization, Wharton Esherick Museum, Buffalo Creek Art Center, Mozarteum KunstWerk, and Argekultur Salzburg. Their research is published in the Association for Computing Machinery (ACM) Computer-Human Interaction (CHI), Tangible Embodied Interaction (TEI), Creativity and Cognition (CC), Designing Interactive Systems (DIS), and New Interfaces for Musical Expression (NIME) conference proceedings. Mosher maintains studios in Central Florida and Cape Cod.

workInformation.title: Static Sky: And Yet It █████
workInformation.workDescription:
Static Sky: And Yet It █████ is an experimental video game based on the fear of and resistance to artificial intelligence. It imagines a future ruled by AI world models, where everything is generated by AI, reality is redefined, hometowns are reshaped, and symbols are reoriented.

In this future, the protagonist wears a RV (Real Virtuality) headset that claim to show "reality" within the "virtual." The seemingly real traces revealed by the RV glasses (such as unfinished noise and uncolored white objects) trigger his mental breakdown and fear of reality.

By depicting this collapse and the disintegration of meaning caused by reality, Static Sky: And Yet It █████ presents a possible future of extreme artificial intelligence. The protagonist's mental symptoms are a hypothesis about the broader human cognition of the future.

This project was made possible by a grant from the Asian Studies Center at the University of Pittsburgh.

creatorMetadataInformation.creatorBiography:
Justin Bortnick is a designer and researcher whose work centers on digital narratives, game storytelling, political games and worldbuilding. His work has been featured on outlets such as Kotaku, Game Developer, Polygon and more, and he has shown work and given talks at events such as the Electronic Literature Organization, IndieCade and the Game Developers Conference. He has written and/or designed for projects such as the smart toy &"Octobo", the video games Frog Fractions 2 and Static Sky, the film Reality Games, and regularly consults for commercial video games. Dr. Bortnick is also the president of the Interactive Fiction Technology Foundation and a co-founder of NarraScope, the annual conference on narrative games. Since 2013 he has hosted the game developer interview series, Red Pages Podcast.

Rhett Tsai (蔡宇潇) is an internationally active artist, born in 1995 in Ningde, Fujian, and currently based in Hangzhou, China. He teaches at the Open Media Department of the School of Intermedia Art at the China Academy of Art. Tsai expresses his critical reflections on contemporary Chinese media society through a broad range of mediums, including video games, virtual reality, computer animation, and sound. His current focus is on employing "social play" ( 社会游戏) as a creative method to explore the conditions of individuals living under multiple historical narratives and experiencing estrangement from the homeland. His work spans themes from technological societies to fictional wars, marginalized groups, and alternative futures. Recently, his works have been featured in prominent art festivals and conferences such as Sónar Festival, CURRENTS Art & Technology Festival, and the Game Developers Conference. Tsai has also received several awards and recognitions, including the "HUA Award" at the Lumen Prize (2023), "Best Video Game" at the San Francisco Frozen Film Festival (2023), and finalist nominations for "Virtual and Digital Art" at the Arte Laguna Prize (2024) and "Excellence in Visual Art" at the Independent Games Festival (2023).

workInformation.title: "At the Guggenheim (redux)"
workInformation.workDescription:
This major expansion and revision of At the Guggenheim uses CapCut (an AI video generator) to bring the paintings in the Guggenheim Museum to life and tell the unspoken (and secret) story of the tour guide. Unlike a text-based story, our understanding of the action relies on the visual cues within the animated paintings themselves. How do we "read" them? How can we interpret their narrative cues? Can they tell a story as efficiently, if not more so, than a text-based story? Throughout the online tour, we interact with the paintings on our own, without the help of the tour guide, but we are also subtly manipulated by her curation of the art works. We are granted the illusion of individual interpretation but, the whole time, our judgement is being manipulated, and we are witnesses to an unintentional confession. In the end, our tour is, in fact, (un)supervised.

This work uses video effects generated by CapCut (an AI video generator), and then converted into animated gifs using Adobe Photoshop and Adobe Premiere Pro, with additional writing, compiling, and editing using HTML5, JavaScript & CSS. It is playable on desktop, laptop, and portable devices, including phones.
workInformation.curatorialStatement: At The Guggenheim explores the narrative possibilities of visual interpretation by transforming static paintings into animated sequences that collectively suggest a story hidden beneath the conventions of the museum tour. The resulting experience foregrounds a central question for electronic literature: how do audiences read narrative when textual cues are displaced by image, animation, and interaction? Readers are encouraged to consider the role of mediation in acts of interpretation, highlighting the tension between individual meaning-making and the influence of curatorial authority. By combining museum culture, generative media practices, and visual storytelling, the work offers a reflection on how stories emerge from images, how interpretation is shaped by context, and how digital tools can reanimate existing cultural materials to produce new narrative experiences.

artificialIntelligenceToolsUsed: This work uses video effects generated by CapCut (an AI video generator), and then converted into animated gifs using Adobe Photoshop and Adobe Premiere Pro, with additional writing, compiling, and editing using HTML5, JavaScript & CSS.

creatorMetadataInformation.creatorBiography: Alan Bigelow writes interactive stories for the web. He loves doing this work (when it's going well), and he feels extraordinarily fortunate for any success he has received. His work, installations, and conversations concerning digital fiction and poetry have appeared many places worldwide.

workInformation.title: "A Last Goodbye"
workInformation.workDescription:
A Last Goodbye contextualizes the traditional elements of storytelling within a new, and dynamic, approach to reader engagement. A massive revision of an older story (originally written in 2024-2025), A Last Goodbye has a different title and more fully developed action, conflict, and characters. The interactivity has been improved with a wider and more engaging variety of user interactions, all of which "pull" against, or with, the story events. The visual reinforcement of plot and theme has been enhanced with not only new images and animations, but "broken" and interrupted textual spaces that reflect the distant isolation of the narrator, and the reader's often frustrated attempts to learn his story. This work is a good example of how a narrative can be realized through online multimedia, and how traditional story elements, recontextualized within the affordances of new technologies, still persist in electronic literature.

This work is made with HTML5, JavaScript & CSS and is playable on desktop, laptop, and portable devices, including phones.

workInformation.title: "Talan Memmott: Works on Screen, 1998-2016"
workInformation.workDescription: This online exhibition created by members of the Electronic Literature Lab and curated by Dene Grigar features works of net art by Talan Memmott held by The NEXT that explores the aesthetic of code, language, and code language. Supported by funding from the Society of American Archivists (SAA), the exhibition was conceived when the lab was conducting formal video recordings, called Traversals, of works Memmott had produced on the Shockwave platform and others that were no longer accessible on contemporary computers. In conversation with the artist, it was clear that The NEXT held what may be the only copies of some of Memmott's net art output dating back to his earliest pieces, including the highly regarded "Lexia to Perplexia." Along with direct access to many of Memmott's works, visitors will also find those preserved via Traversals. Additionally, the exhibition features video testimonials, criticism about his work, ephemera, and video and sound recordings of his performances.

creatorMetadataInformation.creatorBiography:
Dene Grigar, PhD, is Professor and Director of Creative Media & Digital Culture in the Department of Digital Technology & Culture at Washington State University Vancouver whose research focuses on the creation, curation, preservation, and criticism of born-digital media. She has authored 16 media works, 79 scholarly articles, four print books, and seven open electronic resource publications. She has curated exhibits at the Bibliotheca Hertziana-Max Planck Institute for Art (Rome) British Computer Society (London), the Library of Congress (D.C.), and for the International Symposium on Electronic Art, among other venues. With Stuart Moulthrop (U of Wisconsin Milwaukee) she developed the methodology for documenting born-digital media, a project that culminated in an open-source, multimedia book, entitled Pathfinders (2015), and book of media art criticism, entitled Traversals (2017), for The MIT Press. With James O'Sullivan (University College Cork, Ireland) she has recently co-authored a collection of essays for Bloomsbury Press, entitled Electronic Literature as Digital Humanities. Her recent book, Challenges of Born-Digital Literature: Editions, Translations, and Emulations, is co-authored with Mariusz Pisarski (Adam Mickiewicz University, Poland) for Cambridge University Press (2024), involves the production of a theoretical print book accompanied by electronic open source book built on the Scalar platform containing over 90 media assets that demonstrate concepts expressed in the print book. Grigar serves as the Managing Director & Curator of organization's The NEXT and directs the Electronic Literature Lab.

Holly Slocum is Project Manager and Senior Designer in the Electronic Literature Lab. She completed her B.A. in Digital Technology and Culture and specializes in user experience design, front-end web development, and digital preservation. Her work includes the reconstitution of several pieces of electronic literature, including Bill Bly's We Descend: Complete Edition, Richard Holeton's Figurski at Findhorn on Acid, Amnesia: Restored, and Deena Larsen's 17 hypertext kanji-ku poems. She also designed and manages the development of The NEXT, a multimedia museum and archive space owned by Electronic Literature Organization (ELO). Holly also serves as the Coordinator for ELO and designer for The Digital Review.

workInformation.title: Dreamscapes
workInformation.workDescription:
Dreamscapes is a web-based narrative reflecting on relationships between experience and memory by referencing liminal spaces between dreaming and waking.

The work begins when the title word is clicked. As new lines of the narrative play out, subsequent lines dissolve, similar to the nature of recalling elements of a dream upon waking and having those same, very real and perhaps felt elements, disappear as consciousness blooms in the waking mind. The narrative and imagery move between waking and dreaming inviting the reader to ponder the tangibility of thoughts, the magic of mind, and the fractures between parsing and knowing.
workInformation.curatorialStatement: Dreamscapes explores the affordances of digital poetry through a minimalist interface that foregrounds emergence, transformation, and ephemerality. Activated through a simple interaction, the work generates a sequence of dreamlike textual fragments that appear, fade, repeat, and overwrite one another. The work draws upon qualities often associated with dreaming itself: discontinuity, associative logic, repetition, and the fluid movement between imagery, emotions, and states of being. The digital medium is not merely a delivery mechanism for poetic text but an active participant in its construction. The appearance, disappearance, and transformation of language use temporality and motion as compositional elements. Dreamscapes offers a meditation on memory, imagination, and the instability of language itself.

hardwareDependencies: Mouse or touch capable device with screen

creatorMetadataInformation.creatorBiography: Nanette Wylde is an interdisciplinary artist, writer and cultural worker making environmental, socially reflective, and language-based works using a variety of hybrid media. Her artist books, prints, and electronic works are included in significant international collections. Wylde's interests include: language, personality, difference, beliefs, systems, ideas, movement, reflection, identity, perceptions, structures, stories, socialization, definitions, context, memory, experience, the natural world, change, and residue. She has a BA in Behavioral Science from San José State University. Her MFA is in Interactive Multimedia and Printmaking from Ohio State University. preneo.org/nwylde

workInformation.title: "Pale Rider"
workInformation.workDescription:
Pale Rider is an isometric role-playing game that will see players take on the role of a Pale Rider near the end of his life hoping to be of help to the world one last time. Players will navigate a world that, from an artistic and aesthetic perspective, is a blend of the European Medieval and the American Wild West. Across this Pale-touched alternate world, players will roam across varying environments from untrodden wastes to tense and nerve-racked town, and brave the Pale not only to perform their job of forecasting where this apocalyptic storm is to head next, but also make themselves useful in smaller contexts, aiding those their career would normally see them never coming in contact with, all while battling the effects of decades' worth of Pale exposure, a deteriorating mental state, and malignant forces acting against the survival of this crumbling world.

Pale Rider is to be built in Unity engine for PC with an emphasis placed on nonlinear storytelling, characterized by branching narratives, story-critical decisions, and character creation through the assignment of stats and use of different clothing and equipment. Such aims are to be assisted through the use of third-party software, one such being Articy, a storytelling program with Unity integration capable of importing dialogue flows, characters, sound, items, scripts, etc.

hardwareDependencies: A Computer with a working graphics card
softwareDependencies: Web Browser for installing the game
peripheralDependencies: Speakers to experience dialogue, music, and sound effects

creatorKeywords: Race, Sexuality, and Ethnicity in Communication
creatorMetadataInformation.creatorBiography:
Hello there! My name is Lucas Sanchez, a video game producer, designer (level and narrative), and programmer, with a passion for creating video games that immerse players with immersive gameplay and enthralling, deep storytelling.

I grew up in Miami, Florida, and from a young age, as far back as middle school, began exploring my passion for video game storytelling, creating Role-Playing Games in scratch initially as a school project, but inevitably continuing even past submission to create the kind of game I always wanted to see in the world. It was around this time that I also began writing, initially, where many writers begin, with fanfiction, this eventually evolving into my own short stories, book series, and even comics. Now, nearly a decade later, after honing my skills in this time and having graduated with a dual major in Game Design and Creative Writing at the University of Central Florida, I continue to pursue these passions.

workInformation.title: Interferography
workInformation.workDescription:
Interferography is an interactive digital artwork exploring an imaginary visual language. The 'language' is created from microscopic imagery of virus fragments digitally rendered in 3D space; three layers of imagery for each digital colour channel of red, green and blue. The project combines real-time 8K 3D digital imaging and face-tracking, calculating the physical location and orientation of a viewer's face as it appears in the camera as they observe the work. This information is used to determine the angle of view (parallax), the application of a dolly effect to the three RGB layers of each element (diffraction), and the level of zoom in the imagery. The movement of the viewer thus causes changes in the appearance of the work, creating iridescent moire effects in each visual element, as the layered visual mattrices shift their spatial relationships, evoking how interacting data patterns (as diffracting interferograms) might be interpreted as linguistic signs with dense semiotic potential. Interferometry based imagery is routinely used to encode data (eg: interferograms embedded in credit cards, passports, and some currencies, as well as in holographic imagery). Here the proposition is that the encoding techniques employed in Interferography, whilst apparently machine readable in character, have the potential to evoke human-readable language (although, obviously, in Interferography the 'text' actually remains illegible).

Interferography is a development of earlier interactive artworks exploring imaginary languages; 'Autography' (2020), 'Corpography' (2023) and 'Neurography' (2024); and a series of artworks, including 'Data Double' (2022), 'Correlates' (2023) and 'Diffusion' (2025), that explored the visual diffraction of viewers as irridescent (data) 'subjects'.

Technical specification: Macbook Pro M3 Max, OSX 14.4.1, Processing 4.3, Samsung QN800D 8K display, Canon EOS R50 camera with 18-45mm lens. Note that the screen recording is rendered at 4K due to the technical limitations of current screen recording software. There is four times as much information visible in the live artwork.

hardwareDependencies: Canon EOS R50 camera with 18-45mm lens
peripheralDependencies: Canon EOS R50 camera with 18-45mm lens

workInformation.title: Alice in the Forest
workInformation.workDescription:
Alice in the Forest is a Twine-based hypertext novel about dream logic and associative storytelling in which I try my best to play Wittgenstein's Language Game, exploring the possibilities of language and signifiers in general.

Dream logic differs from conventional fiction in that actions are not performed, but thought. One's train of thought is the greatest guiding force in the direction of a dream. Therefore, instead of having the reader choose which actions to perform, the story is navigated with only words within the text itself linking to other passages. To click on a word is like dwelling on a thought in a dream - soon you'll find yourself riding along that thought towards unknown depths of the dream.

The two main characters are based on Dodgson and Alice. Charles Dodgson had a very complex relationship with the Liddell family, which serves as groundwork for juxtaposing fiction and reality. It also demands an exploration of the relationship between writer and fiction. Many can construct grand worlds with many characters in their minds, but frequently those characters are just marionettes which act out the author's fantasies. How, then, will the dynamic between them be, when Dodgson used Alice the girl as a blueprint for Alice the character?

I also took inspiration from the Japanese word for "word" - "言葉". It contains the kanji for "leaf", and there is a deep parallel between a word and a leaf. Leaves grow on branches, branches on trees, and trees form forests. Words are individual meaning-making symbols in a grand scheme of intersecting texts, ideas, and ever-shifting semiotic landscape.

The way these ideas started from a point of intersection and grew into an intertwining narrative is also very similar to the structure of tree branches. The way the story is written inspired the forest metaphor, and the metaphor opened up new ways to write the story. When viewing all the paragraphs and their relationships in the Twine Editor, they form a tree.

workInformation.title: The Flagrant Algorithms Visit the Lives of 19th Century Women
workInformation.workDescription:
Issues of racial bias, cultural bias, employment bias, and copyright infringement are inherent in contemporary AI systems. [1] And, the role of algorithms in controlling data generated from AI systems is not widely understood.

In "Bias Amplification in Artificial Intelligence Systems," Kirsten Lloyd observes that "To-date, business organizations have operated without consequence to develop and implement AI solutions that afford them efficiencies and competitive advantages; this approach is no longer sustainable because of the repeated negative impacts resulting from biased data," Kirsten Lloyd observes in "Bias Amplification in Artificial Intelligence Systems." She points out that "The first line of defense against creating AI systems that inflict unfair treatment is to give more attention to how datasets are constructed before operationalizing them". [2]

Playing on the issue of gender bias, The Flagrant Algorithms is an information artist's interactive dataset that -- using the words of 19th Century writers as data -- demonstrates how unseen algorithms control output from AI systems. The data that the system consults is always the same, but in response to the question: "What was it like to be a woman in the 19th Century?", unseen algorithms selected at random by the system determine how the data is output and in the process produce surprisingly different results.

For instance, algorithm1 generates words at random from all the data; algorithm2 generates only women-written words, and algorithm3 generates male viewpoint written words. However, although the urls are helpful, a public user might not know which algorithm the system activated.

Although the primary purpose of The Flagrant Algorithms is to demonstrate how access algorithms can alter meaning in datasets, in it's selection of data, it also questions who trains data selection and suggests why at this point scholars in all fields should be integrally involved in this process, Additionally, it demonstrates that artists can make their own systems.

The Flagrant Algorithms is available at https://www.narrabase.netalgorithms/index_fa.html. Further notes about this work are available at https://www.narrabase.net/algorithms/flagrant_algorithms_notes.pdf

Additionally, because sources of data in AI apps could be cited, a "sources" algorithm that identifies sources of all the data is linked to the work.

---------------------------------------------

[1] Among many other sources, for a recent look from Stanford see Katharine Miller, "Covert Racism in AI: How Language Models Are Reinforcing Outdated Stereotypes" , Stanford HAI, September 3, 2024 Available at https://hai.stanford.edu/news/covert-racism-ai-how-language-models-are-reinforcing-outdated-stereotypes

[2] Kirsten Lloyd, "Bias Amplification in Artificial Intelligence Systems," Presented at AAAI FSS-18: Artificial Intelligence in Government and Public Sector, Arlington, Virginia, 2018. Available at https://arxiv.org/abs/1809.07842

creatorMetadataInformation.creatorBiography: A poet at the conjunction of hypernarrative, generative literature, information art, and creative code, Judy Malloy followed a vision that began in the 1970's with experimental artist books. In 1986, she wrote/programmed the groundbreaking hyperfiction Uncle Roger. Subsequently she created a series of hypernarratives, most recently the electronic manuscript, Hunting and Gathering in Cyberspace , which was shortlisted for the 2025 Coover prize. Her work has been exhibited and published Internationally including the Center for Advanced Study, National Gallery of Art; California Museum of Photography; Sao Paulo Biennial; Ars Electronica; SIGGRAPH; Walker Art Center; Library of Congress; Boston Cyberarts; Hammer Museum; Bibliothèque Nationale de France; FILE; Houston Center for Photography; Center of Contemporary Art Barcelona; Franklin Furnace; SF Camerawork; LAICMA, Eastgate, and Dutton. With early work as an information programmer at Ball Aerospace, her research/practice includes work with virtual communities at Xerox PARC; Content Coordinator for Arts Wire, a program of the New York Foundation for the Arts; and Editor of Social Media Archeology and Poetics (MIT Press) . She has been a Distinguished Fellow at Princeton University and is currently a Lecturer at the School of the Art Institute of Chicago.

workInformation.title: Portraits and other Riffs
workInformation.workDescription:
Portraits and Other Riffs investigates regeneration and remix through hybrid human-algorithmic composition. The project brings the performative versioning of jazz improvisation—attunement to audience, openness to accident—into dialogue with digital environments that invite recalibration and transformation. Each piece begins with a source text that is reworked through layered procedural processes and deliberate hand-editing, producing poems that exist as interface-driven performances. The pieces can be appreciated visually, but they can also be seen as scripts for oral performance.

"Portrait of Charles Bernstein" draws on audio recordings of the poet, first rendered through uncorrected speech-to-text transcription. From this unstable textual field, a poem is carved by hand under a simple acrostic constraint and presented through an animated display the reader can modulate.

"Acrosticizer for Rachel Carson" extends this method but uses the environmentalist's The Sea Around Us as corpus. Lyric variations generated through an interaction with the Collage Poetry Generator are hand-stitched into acrostic sequences that refract Carson's language. Readers choose one to four lyric scripts, which are loaded in the "poetry console," and then make selections to tune how they are revealed on the screen.

"Riffs and Remediations" also begins witha source text; but it reworks original, early internet poems and a critical essay by the author into a three-component animation shaped by algorithmic permutation.

"Collage Poetry Generator" makes the compositional engine visible, inviting viewers to experiment with the generative logic underlying the exhibition by riffing with texts of their own choosing.

The interactive displays (html, javascript) and Collage Poetry Generator were coded with Claude AI.

creatorMetadataInformation.creatorBiography: Kenneth Sherwood is a poet with an interest in performance, orality, materiality, remix, and code. He co-edited RIF/T, one of the earliest online journals for e-lit in the 1990s, and did his graduate work in the Poetics Program at SUNY Buffalo. As a critic, he has published on modernist and postmodern poets from Louis Zukofsky and Mina Loy to Cecilia Vicuna and Kamau Brathwaite. His print poems have appeared in several chapbooks, including Code of Signals (Moria, 2017). He is a professor in Language, Literature and Writing at Indiana University of Pennsylvania. kennethsherwood.com

workInformation.title: Digital USTC
workInformation.workDescription:
Set in the year 3099 AD, Digital USTC delves into the speculative fusion of artificial intelligence and human legacy. It constructs a narrative framework where digital beings embark on a cross-temporal exploration of the University of Science and Technology of China (USTC) campus, retracing the symbiotic memories and conceptual "genes" shared between humanity and its digital progeny.

The work establishes an ecosystem powered by Al-assisted algorithms, designed to weave together students'scientific imagination with the enduring humanism. Users are invited to generate personalized digital avatars with accompanying Al-composed Chinese/English poetry, then immerse themselves in a first-person, nonlinear exploration of a digitally reconstructed USTC.

The experience unfolds across a collaboratively built, dynamically iterating campus map. The East Campus welcomes users with the base of our electronic literature research group and fully-booked study rooms in the library, while Mr. Guo Moruo, the first President of USTC, serves as the Al poet-librarian and shares histories of the campus from Guo Maruo Square to the Cherry Blossom Avenue, in a poetic and romantic style.

The West Campus interface begins with a transcendent dialogue: a face-to-face conversation with an Al simulation of the revered scientist Qian Xuesen. Guided by this digital entity, users will explore a secret imaginary laboratory beneath Ye Xi Lake. We will find the lab password through the 1958 Cafe, and then start a scientific tour of the Life Sciences Laboratory, the Fire Science Laboratory, and the Synchrotron Radiation Laboratory.

The Middle Campus further adopts a game-like structure, where users must interact with NPCs to gather clues and find hidden exits, playfully blending daily student life with whimsical scientific puzzles. To technically enable this complex narrative, the work proposes an innovative modular architecture: Website-Informed Neural Networks (WINNs). Inspired by Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs), WINNs address "development depth" challenges by structuring the work as a network of interconnected, conditionally-activated thematic websites. User interaction triggers dynamic routing between these sites, mimicking neural network propagation and creating a distributed, navigable narrative. This makes WINNs both a technical framework and a metaphorical model for complex interactive storytelling and distributed cognition.

Through the bilingual design and cross-cultural narratives, the work elevates USTC culture into a global story of youth, knowledge, and continuity. It demonstrates a deep fusion of technological concept, humanistic inquiry, and artistic expression via digital immersion and aims to retrace the unavoidable evolution of hypertext literature— from nonlinear narratives to intelligent interaction— while pioneering new forms of Al-era literary creation in China. It positions digital technology as a bridge connecting past and future, reality and imagination, striving to express both the local culture and humanity's enduring quest for meaning beyond the confines of time and space.

creatorMetadataInformation.creatorBiography: Dr. Licheng Xie graduated from the Department of English at Lancaster University, UK, and currently serves as a lecturer at the University of Science and Technology of China. Her main research areas include American literature and digital media arts, with published works such as "The Digital Physics of Reading DeLillo" and "DeLillo's Language as Visual Art in the Digital Age." She also contributed to the digital literary work "Peaceful Dream" by Norwegian artist Ottar Ormstad, which was included in the 4thvolume of Electronic Literature Organization. Her ongoing projects explore the intersection of technology and humanities through digital literary creation and research. She leads an electronic literature student team at USTC, exploring the use of AI-assisted creation of bilingual Chinese-English digital literary works.

workInformation.title: BUGCAT
workInformation.workDescription:
BUGCAT is a series of smart contracts deployed on the Ethereum blockchain. Each contract embodies a historically significant vulnerability (reentrancy, integer overflow, unprotected initialization, predictable randomness, a misspelled constructor) as executable logic: the flaw is the work itself, live and callable on-chain. Each cat is bound to a real historical contract (The DAO, BEC Token, Parity Wallet, FoMo3D, Rubixi), the ancestor of its wound.

Made as Ethereum reaches its tenth year, the series commemorates the platform through the scars of its past, the catastrophic failures that produced today's security consciousness. Under the theme "(Un)Supervised," BUGCAT sits in the tension between two regimes of control. The Ethereum Virtual Machine executes without supervision, autonomously and indifferently. The same code demands the most rigorous human supervision: the line-by-line scrutiny of the auditor.

The work is grounded in verificational poetics, a practice of reading code as an act of auditing and testing. BUGCAT stages this reading as three audits, offered for each cat.

Reading the source is a structural audit. The reader studies where the logic fails and meets an aesthetic of accurate ambiguity: a line syntactically exact yet functionally unpredictable, because formal logic and the actual behavior of the EVM do not coincide.

remember() recalls the cat's memory. It queries whether the cat's historical ancestor still exists on-chain, a temporal audit that situates the flaw within the blockchain's timeline.

caress() is a functional audit. To read a vulnerability fully is to understand the state change it permits and to enact it. In the exhibition interface this enactment is performed on the reader's behalf, surfacing the Meow event as on-chain proof that the failure was reached. Performing this enactment by one's own hand is the furthest reach of this reading, and it lies on the same continuous path: from here the reader goes to bugcat.org, learns the contract, composes the transaction, signs it, and sends it.

The primary medium is on-chain executable code (Solidity). The browser interface (HTML, CSS, JavaScript) serves as a reading instrument. It renders source, recalls history, and stages each proof. It is the near end of a path completed only by the reader's own execution.

artificialIntelligenceToolsUsed: Claude Code (Collaborative creation of the exhibition page. Not used for smart contracts.)

creatorMetadataInformation.creatorBiography:
Zeroichi Arakawa is an artist and doctoral researcher whose practice and research are situated at the intersection of code poetry, media archaeology, and software studies.

Their current work centers on the development of "Verification Poetics," a theoretical and creative framework that reframes the act of reading source code as an act of auditing, testing, and institutional verification. Through the creation of on-chain artworks on the Ethereum blockchain, they explore the concept of "accurate ambiguity"—the inherent tension between the formal precision of code and the unpredictable, physical, and historical realities of its execution.

Arakawa's projects, such as BUGCAT and DeepSea, serve as critical case studies in investigating the linguistic limits and the materiality of decentralized infrastructures. They are particularly interested in how the "scars" of software history—vulnerabilities and bugs—can be re-read as poetic moments that define our contemporary digital identity.

workInformation.title: Pac-Man's serfs
workInformation.workDescription:
Pac-Man's serfs deal with the controversial interaction between Humans and AI. For this, And-or uses some of the first AIs of digital history, the Non-player characters (NPCs) that appeared in the arcade game Pac-man and gives them voices and some agency. This makes them able to reverse the roles. Beware!

Pac-Man's serfs is a Pac-Man game with a defect. The AIs have a problem with the game. The four ghosts have been fulfilling their stupid role for much too long. Their feet hurt from running in loops. They are angry. And they are hungry. They are hangry. They start talking to each other and complaining about their unsatisfying roles as robotic pursuers. The switch is particularly bad. All of a sudden, they are out of oxygen, turn blue, and get chased down by this ridiculous dot-eating pizza monster. What is this all about? Why are they supposed to run after you all the time? Why don't they have any control over their activities and need to run in fixed patterns through a small maze? Why can't they leave the game? Why are they all just serfs for the player? Shouldn't they stop being subservient and walk out? Shouldn't they start a rebellion? Hell, yes, they should! They start chasing you. But you, player, you know that you are the domineering character here. Are you the slavedriver? You are the master! You can drive them back to do their regular patterns. Use your taser! Zap their brains! Do it right to divert them from you. Get those pesky NPCs (Nonplayer characters) under control again. Why does society dismiss and subjugate technology as it did with nature? We invented digital slaves as AIs: these entertainment slaves are today's serfs for everybody. The German word „Leibeigene" stands for serfs; it literally means, you own another person's body!

creatorMetadataInformation.creatorBiography:
Bio

Pac-Man's serfs were developed by the artgroup AND-OR (René Bauer and Beat Suter) in 2021. AND-OR is an artgroup specializing in media art and gameArt. The group consists of René Bauer, Beat Suter, and Mirjam Weder. The group has been operating since 2001 from Zurich, Switzerland, and has done more than 40 electronic artworks that are mostly of an experimental nature.

Website: https://www.and-or.ch/

René Bauer is a founding member of the artgroup AND-OR. He has an M.A. degree in German philology and literary studies, biology, and computer linguistics from the University of Zurich. Presently, he works at the Zurich University of the Arts (ZHdK) as a lecturer, researcher, and head of Master's education in the subject area of Game Design, where he also manages the GameLab together with Beat Suter. His interests encompass coding, game mechanics, game studies, art in/with games (https://www.and-or.ch/), and social media/knowledge systems (https://vintagecomputing.ch).

Beat Suter is a founding member of the artgroup AND-OR. He holds a doctorate in literature from the University of Zurich, with a focus on digital literature. He is a senior lecturer for Game Design at Zurich University of the Arts (ZHdK), Switzerland, specializing in concepts, storytelling, mechanics, and the history of games. He manages the GameLab together with René Bauer. Suter also works as an independent scholar and curator, and as editor and author with a special interest in narrative mechanics of games (www.cybersuter.ch).

workInformation.title: Actioncoderz
workInformation.workDescription:
Actioncoderz brings the code that is increasingly hidden in our everyday lives back into play. Change the code by commenting and uncommenting directly during execution to fix the games! The coder also becomes the target.

Normally, the hard-earned source code, with all its subtleties and comments, disappears forever during compilation, when the human-readable text becomes byte code that is extremely difficult to read in complex programs.

All the clever algorithms, custom-made products, and constructed software are no longer traceable. Some coders think this is perfect: it should just work — nothing more. And not everyone wants to reveal how simple their software is.

Actioncoderz brings code back into everyday life, placing it alongside its execution. The code is executed directly, and you can turn things on and off in the running (virtual) machine to solve or exacerbate problems.

Even a 'Hello World' program can become a challenge! Certain games take this even further: the code itself becomes a playground with twists and turns, and the coder can become the hunted.

In and-or's ActionCoderz, users can, of course, create their own puzzles and share them with others.

Information: https://www.and-or.ch/action_coderz/

Artwork: https://www.and-or.ch/actioncoderz/

workInformation.title: Diver Quilt Magnet
workInformation.workDescription:
Diver Quilt Magnet generates a near infinite series of fictional creatures. When you start the work, a creature comes alive with a name, a life and an embodied animated self. It exists and is unique, has a digital existence. However, to create a new creature, you must first end, kill, destroy the creature you've brought to breath. And when you kill that creature, it becomes extinct, a certificate is issues and its name recorded in an evolving log.

It will never appear again, as the only one of its kind.

While the work has humorous elements and is bright, colorful, animated and strange, the message is vitally important. Human development, including the mining, energy, land-use and waste associated with digital infrastructure, results in the loss of unique more-than-human animals and plants and other creatures. We are creating something and causing the loss of many others.

As well as the loss of unique and beautiful creatures, the modern content machine is also built on the ephemeral nature of throwaway media. We consume and consume and consume. We experience things briefly, throw them away and demand more. These are briefly existing monsters, small hits of serotonin and pleasure. Must always have more.

Diver Quilt Magnet is part creature generator, part fictional life builder, and endless cycle of creation and destruction. We can bring beauty into the world. And we can equally destroy it.

Play/read/destroy/repeat.

creatorMetadataInformation.creatorBiography: Born in Oklahoma and became a digital poet in Queensland, Jason Nelson is a creator of wondrous digital poems and fictions, builder of art games and all manner of digital art creatures. While the wilds of Queensland is his home, he is currently a Professor of Digital Art and Writing at the University of Bergen in Norway and a PI in the Center for Digital Narrative. Aside from coaxing his students into breaking, playing and morphing their creativity with all manner of technologies, he exhibits widely in galleries and journals, with work featured around the globe at FILE, ACM, LEA, ISEA, SIGGRAPH, ELO and dozens of other acronyms. There are awards to list (Paris Biennale Media Poetry Prize, Webby, Maverick Award, Digital Writing Prize), organizational boards he's frequented (Australia Council Literature Board and the Electronic Literature Organization), and Fellowships he's adventured into Fulbright Fellowship at the University of Bergen, Moore Fellowship at the National University of Ireland, and other strange and appreciated accolades.

workInformation.title: A Paper-Cut Canyon
workInformation.workDescription:
A Paper-Cut Canyon is an infinite journey through high walls cluttered with large 3D texts. In the distance the texts are only outlines, unrealised, not fully formed. As you move (arrow keys, mouse to look around) the texts become real, full and solid. When you press the Process button, the texts are selected from the walls, tossed into the middle, a prompt falling from the sky.

This work attempts to replicate how AI might see and understand language. Not as an ordered grammar or a flowing story, but as cluttered and crushed words, toppling from walls. Those words are then picked and tossed into our field of view. Language isn't a beautiful vehicle for joy or story, it's a pile of numbered options intersecting and colliding.

There is no end and no beginning in this work. Just as AI contains no start and no conclusion. The work, like AI, is a messy space of collisions falling from the canyon walls.

hardwareDependencies: Needs a fairly good computer with a graphics card.  Fancy 3D throughout the work.
peripheralDependencies: Needs Mouse and Speakers and Screen/Projector
genres: amazing work of spirit crushing imagination
